content:
# Set Theory

## What is it — Intuitive explanation

A set is a **well-defined collection of distinct objects**. For example, "the collection of integers from 1 to 5" or "the collection of prefectures in Japan" — whenever it is perfectly clear what is and is not included, we call that collection a set. The individual objects belonging to a set are called **elements** (or **members**).

Sets are written using curly braces $\{\}$. For example, we write $A = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. The fact that an element $a$ belongs to a set $A$ is written $a \in A$, and that it does not belong is written $a \notin A$. This clear-cut "either it belongs or it doesn't" quality is the very starting point of set theory.

## Why it matters — Its place in mathematics

Set theory is the **common language** of modern mathematics. Numbers, functions, spaces, probability — virtually every mathematical concept is rigorously defined in terms of sets. For instance, a "function" is defined as a special correspondence between two sets, and "the collection of all natural numbers" is itself constructed as a set with particular properties. Learning set theory is equivalent to understanding the foundation on which the entire edifice of mathematics rests.

Ever since Georg Cantor founded set theory in the late 19th century, the foundations of mathematics have been built on this framework. Fluency with the language of sets allows us to unify arguments across different fields of mathematics and even to compare the "sizes" of infinite collections. The reason set notation appears throughout every subject in university mathematics is simply that sets lie at the very core of the discipline.

## The core idea — Definitions and key results

The most fundamental relationship between sets is that of a **subset**. When every element of a set $A$ also belongs to a set $B$, we write $A \subseteq B$ and say "$A$ is a subset of $B$." When both $A \subseteq B$ and $B \subseteq A$ hold, we define $A = B$.

The three basic set operations are:

$$A \cup B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ or } x \in B\} \quad \text{(union)}$$

$$A \cap B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ and } x \in B\} \quad \text{(intersection)}$$

$$A \setminus B = \{x \mid x \in A \text{ and } x \notin B\} \quad \text{(set difference)}$$

Given a universal set $U$, the **complement** of $A$ is $A^c = U \setminus A$. These operations satisfy elegant laws that mirror those of logic, including De Morgan's laws: $(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$ and $(A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c$.

## Concrete examples — Everyday illustrations

**Example 1: Students and hobbies.** In a class, let $A$ = "the set of students who like music" and $B$ = "the set of students who like sports." Then $A \cup B$ represents "students who like music or sports," $A \cap B$ represents "students who like both," and $A \setminus B$ represents "students who like music but not sports." Drawing a Venn diagram makes each of these regions immediately visible.

**Example 2: The hierarchy of number sets.** Writing the natural numbers, integers, rational numbers, and real numbers as $\mathbb{N}, \mathbb{Z}, \mathbb{Q}, \mathbb{R}$, we have the chain of inclusions
$$\mathbb{N} \subseteq \mathbb{Z} \subseteq \mathbb{Q} \subseteq \mathbb{R}.$$
The seemingly obvious fact that "every integer is also a rational number" is expressed precisely as a subset relationship. In this way, the language of sets gives us a clear and systematic picture of how our number systems are structured.

## Connections — Relationships with other concepts

Set theory is deeply intertwined with **logic**. Union corresponds to the logical "or ($\lor$)," intersection to "and ($\land$)," and complement to "not ($\lnot$)." The laws governing set operations and the laws of propositional logic share exactly the same structure.

Defining **maps (functions)** between sets provides the foundation for algebra, analysis, and topology. When a bijection exists between two sets, they are considered to have the "same size," which leads to Cantor's concept of **cardinality**. It can be shown, for instance, that the set of all natural numbers and the set of all integers have the same cardinality, while the set of all real numbers is a "larger" infinite set.

## Applications — How this concept is used in physics, chemistry, economics, engineering, and beyond

**In probability theory and statistics**, the "events" of a probability space are defined as sets. In a dice-rolling experiment, the event "an even number appears" is the set $\{2, 4, 6\}$, and union and intersection correspond directly to calculating the probability that "at least one event occurs" or that "both events occur simultaneously." Data filtering and join operations in data science (such as SQL JOINs) are likewise set operations at their core.

**In computer science and engineering**, database queries and the type systems of programming languages are described in the language of set theory. Logic circuit design is based on Boolean algebra — an algebraic system isomorphic to set operations — where AND, OR, and NOT gates correspond to intersection, union, and complement, respectively. **In economics**, sets are the standard tool for formalizing the behavior of economic agents: preference relations, budget sets, and strategy sets in game theory are all naturally expressed as sets.

terms.definition: A collection of clearly distinguishable objects. It is precisely determined what belongs to it and what does not.
An individual object that makes up a set. $a \in A$ means "$a$ is an element of set $A$."
When every element of set $A$ also belongs to set $B$, we write $A \subseteq B$ and say "$A$ is a subset of $B$."
$A \cup B$: The set of all elements that belong to at least one of $A$ or $B$.
$A \cap B$: The set of all elements that belong to both $A$ and $B$.
With respect to the universal set $U$, $A^c = U \setminus A$: the set of all elements that belong to $U$ but not to $A$.
$A \setminus B$: The set of all elements that belong to $A$ but not to $B$.
A set that contains no elements. Denoted $\emptyset$ or $\{\}$. It is a subset of every set.
A concept representing the "size" of a set. For finite sets it corresponds to the number of elements, and it is also used to compare the sizes of infinite sets.
The laws stating that $(A \cup B)^c = A^c \cap B^c$ and $(A \cap B)^c = A^c \cup B^c$. An important property connecting set operations and logical operations.

diagrams.name: concept-diagram.svg
diagrams.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 600 420" width="600" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="system-ui, sans-serif">
  <!-- No background (transparent) -->

  <!-- Title -->
  <text x="300" y="30" text-anchor="middle" font-size="16" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Basic Set Operations (Venn Diagram)</text>

  <!-- ===== Universal Set Frames ===== -->
  <!-- Top left: Union -->
  <rect x="20" y="50" width="170" height="130" rx="8" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/>
  <text x="105" y="45" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Universal Set U</text>
  <!-- A circle -->
  <circle cx="80" cy="115" r="40" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.25" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- B circle -->
  <circle cx="115" cy="115" r="40" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.25" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="65" y="118" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">A</text>
  <text x="132" y="118" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">B</text>
  <text x="105" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="currentColor">A ∪ B (Union)</text>

  <!-- Top center: Intersection -->
  <rect x="215" y="50" width="170" height="130" rx="8" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/>
  <text x="300" y="45" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Universal Set U</text>
  <!-- A circle (gray) -->
  <circle cx="275" cy="115" r="40" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- B circle (gray) -->
  <circle cx="310" cy="115" r="40" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- intersection highlight -->
  <clipPath id="clipA">
    <circle cx="275" cy="115" r="40"/>
  </clipPath>
  <circle cx="310" cy="115" r="40" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.45" stroke="none" clip-path="url(#clipA)"/>
  <text x="260" y="118" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">A</text>
  <text x="327" y="118" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">B</text>
  <text x="300" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="currentColor">A ∩ B (Intersection)</text>

  <!-- Top right: Complement -->
  <rect x="410" y="50" width="170" height="130" rx="8" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.2" stroke-dasharray="5,3"/>
  <text x="495" y="45" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">Universal Set U</text>
  <!-- U fill -->
  <rect x="410" y="50" width="170" height="130" rx="8" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.18" stroke="none"/>
  <!-- A circle (cut out visually with white-ish) -->
  <circle cx="495" cy="115" r="40" fill="white" fill-opacity="0.55" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- re-draw A circle fill with low opacity currentColor for dark mode compat -->
  <circle cx="495" cy="115" r="40" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.08" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="495" y="118" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">A</text>
  <text x="495" y="195" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="currentColor">Aᶜ (Complement)</text>

  <!-- ===== Bottom: Subset Relationship ===== -->
  <!-- Large circle B -->
  <ellipse cx="150" cy="320" rx="110" ry="70" fill="#22c55e" fill-opacity="0.12" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="1.8"/>
  <text x="215" y="295" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#22c55e">B</text>
  <!-- Medium circle A -->
  <ellipse cx="140" cy="325" rx="70" ry="45" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.18" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1.8"/>
  <text x="175" y="305" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#3b82f6">A</text>
  <!-- Small circle C -->
  <ellipse cx="130" cy="330" rx="38" ry="25" fill="#ef4444" fill-opacity="0.22" stroke="#ef4444" stroke-width="1.8"/>
  <text x="130" y="334" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" font-weight="bold" fill="#ef4444">C</text>
  <!-- Labels -->
  <text x="150" y="405" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="currentColor">Nested Subset Structure</text>
  <text x="150" y="420" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">C ⊆ A ⊆ B</text>

  <!-- ===== Bottom right: Hierarchy of Number Sets ===== -->
  <text x="420" y="230" text-anchor="middle" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="currentColor">Hierarchy of Number Sets</text>
  <!-- ℝ -->
  <ellipse cx="420" cy="320" rx="105" ry="75" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.04" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.3"/>
  <text x="495" y="265" font-size="12" fill="currentColor">ℝ (Real Numbers)</text>
  <!-- ℚ -->
  <ellipse cx="415" cy="325" rx="75" ry="52" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.05" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.3"/>
  <text x="463" y="283" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">ℚ (Rational Numbers)</text>
  <!-- ℤ -->
  <ellipse cx="410" cy="330" rx="48" ry="33" fill="currentColor" fill-opacity="0.07" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1.3"/>
  <text x="430" y="305" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">ℤ (Integers)</text>
  <!-- ℕ -->
  <ellipse cx="405" cy="335" rx="26" ry="18" fill="#3b82f6" fill-opacity="0.25" stroke="#3b82f6" stroke-width="1.3"/>
  <text x="405" y="338" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="currentColor">ℕ</text>
  <text x="420" y="410" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" fill="currentColor">ℕ ⊆ ℤ ⊆ ℚ ⊆ ℝ</text>
</svg>
